speaker: interviewer
text: 28, 1996. This is tape number 4, interviewing Mrs. Sonia Hochman. I'm Marianne Kador. So you suddenly found out that you were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're liberated. We came outside. It was April the 12th, 1942. The ground was still white with snow. In the Ukraine, the winter-- it was a very cold winter in 1944. It was a very, very cold winter. My sister, Sima, went in a year before that. She didn't remember what snow was. And she started to scream, we should close the [INAUDIBLE]. You see, it was very dark in the grotto. So we had, like, a potato, put in a little bit oil and a, and a thread. And this was a light, but a tiny, little light. But it's very dark, shows a big reflection. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, her eyes were damaged from the brightness of the sun, a little bit, and of course, malnutrition. Anyway, we came to our town. We take a look. The entire town was taken apart. The peasants took apart the town. It was a town. All the homes were taken apart because this one needed a roof. The other one needed windows. The third one needed walls. They needed the bricks. And they broke apart all the Jewish homes. There was one Jewish home that a maid had taken over. She used to work for the Jewish home. Anyway, the boys chased out the maid, and we all went in to stay in this home. But a Jewish captain came, and he spoke to my father and to my uncles very quietly. He didn't want--

speaker: interviewer
text: A Polish or Russian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Russian. Russian. But he was Jewish. He spoke to them, and he said look, he says, I'm Jewish. But I don't want anyone to know that I'm Jewish. He spoke very quietly. He didn't want anyone to know. And he said, here, and he gave them in a sack two machine guns and some ammunition. He said get out of this. From here, go to a bigger town. You're not safe here. He knew already that there were Ukrainian bands. We were not aware of it yet. Because there was a General [INAUDIBLE] that left the Russians, and he joined the Germans. And they were fighting in the woods on the, on the, on the German side.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they were not the townspeople. They were wandering bands.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no, no, this was, this was one. He knew about those. But what happened was there were a lot of collaborators, Ukrainian collaborators, Polish collaborators, mostly Ukrainian collaborators of the Germans. And they went into the woods. And who did they start to kill right away? Jews. And evidently he realized that, that there's going to be-- we were not aware of that. You know, when we came out, we were so excited that we were alive that we didn't think of-- if we were safe here or not. And he said, get out. Get away from here. You're not safe.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the same week, a big catastrophe happened. [NON-ENGLISH] Dodek and his wife, [NON-ENGLISH] Dodek had a small, little boy. She went to a peasant-- up where the peasants were to try to get milk for the child. The child was so under-nourished. And she disappeared. She never came back. My uncle, Nisel and [NON-ENGLISH] went to search for her. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They went to search for her, and they came-- the [NON-ENGLISH] was a Ukrainian policeman. He must have killed her. Because when they came in, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the mother started to yell, [NON-ENGLISH]. What do you want here?

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they killed [NON-ENGLISH] Dodek, and they wounded my Uncle Nisel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is just a week or two right after liberation. Two people that survived with us in the grottoes perished.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you imagine the crying of those sister and brothers and that mother, that old mother [NON-ENGLISH] Dodek? She felt [NON-ENGLISH] Dodek, the most gorgeous guy in [NON-ENGLISH], beautiful young people.

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to Borszczow. In Borszczow, they had already a military command, a [NON-ENGLISH]. And we were looking for homes. My father saved the two [NON-ENGLISH] brothers, so we had big homes. And on the other side of the road near the home was the, was the recruiting, where they were recruiting already to the Russian army. As soon as they came in, they started to grab people for the army. My father and mother and I and, and Sima-- my father is unpacking a horse and wagon. He's taking us off the horse and wagon. We take a look, there a Russian soldier comes over, and he takes him. They're taking him away to the army. He didn't unpack the wagon. He's only out two weeks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So in order not to go to the front, he went [NON-ENGLISH]. That means on the working class. He went to the Urals to work in the mines in a slave-- in a, in a, in a labor camp. We're crying again. My father-- they took away my father. During the first Russians, when they came in in '39 to '41, he was hiding. He don't want to go to Siberia. So he could have run away this time, too. But he said to himself he was hiding enough. He'll go to, to Russia. He was a strong man. He was only 34 years old at that time, 34 and a half, almost 35 years old. And after the war, he'll come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, life goes on. My father-- Do you remember when he left?

speaker: interviewee
text: He left as long-- he left, and he said to me, [NON-ENGLISH]. Take care of your mother and your sister, Simala. And I kept that vow to my father. I kept that promise. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- I'm a devoted sister. We love each other. She's devoted to me, too. We love each other so, my sister and I. I don't know if there are any other sisters that love each other that much. We speak to each other-- she lives in Montreal, Canada-- every day. We're on the phone to each other. My mother was sick. She had cancer. I took care of my mother. The doctors used to say, you're like a, a watch dog. We've never seen anyone-- I knew-- I loved my mother, of course. And I knew that it was my responsibility. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father, he was such a nice man. Anyway, I started to go to school. My grandfather started to, started to do business. And you know, whenever you're a little girl, you go to school with other children, with Polish children, Ukrainian children. My mother opened up a little store. They, they allowed her to have a little store since her husband was in the army and she had no way of supporting herself. So she was allowed to sell, like, shoelaces and yeast and-- not big things, you know, but just to make a, a mere living for herself and her children. My grandfather started to-- they took over our house. My grandfather started to deal, you know, wheel again. They bought right away a horse and wagon or they took back a horse and wagon from the, from, from the peasants, whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what town was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Borszczow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Borszczow.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is in the summer. This, I would say, is May, May, May, June '44. The war is still going on. The war is still going on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, my grandfather went to Czernowitz, Romania. The border was very close to us. He went to Czernowitz to do-- to buy things to bring to our town to sell. And he sees Jewish girls are walking. He says meydl, girls, where are you from? And one of them says she's from [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: And she says her name is [NON-ENGLISH]. And this was my grandfather's sister's granddaughter. How he brought her home, and how he cried. And he found another granddaughter of his sister's, [NON-ENGLISH]. Her name is now Silver. She lives in Los Angeles. And [NON-ENGLISH] became my aunt. She married my Uncle Nisel. He brought those two girls in, and they became like daughters in my grandparents' house. And how happy he was that he found two grandchildren of his sister. And things, life is going on. My father's writing us from the Urals. He became already a big shot there. You know, he was a very-- he was such a charismatic person, you know. He had such charisma in him. He was very handsome and very charismatic. I'll show you later. But he was intelligent. And he could-- they used to say that there are two stones sitting there in the field, Fischel could do business with one of them and then, and then sell the other one to the other. That type of a businessman he was. He was, he was fantastic. Anyway, my grandfather is doing business. And this already, we're now at-- and I'm going to school. I'm reciting. And [SIGHS] we're trying to live normal lives. In November 1944, my grandfather takes a horse and wagon with a little Ukrainian boy, and he decides to go to Bilcze to pick up a-- peasant came to town and told him he has, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he has pears for him.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they have Banderists there. This was a Ukrainian nationalist. But who did they kill first? The Jews. They also killed Poles. They killed Russians. But mostly, they killed Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: The few surviving Jews that survived, if they left the town or they would come in, run in at night, and they would shoot through our homes. My grandmother was begging him not to go. It's dangerous. He says he's going to lay down in the back, and the boy is going to take the horse and wagon. And he was always such a cautious man. That day, we don't know. It's like something was pulling him. And he left. I was in school. My sister wasn't in school yet, you know. I was in school. My mother had that little store. And my grandmother was busy. But the rest of the family-- the boy comes back [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] crying, running, that they killed my grandfather.

speaker: interviewee
text: The Banderists who came, and they said, [NON-ENGLISH]. What do you have here? And he says, I have pears in the wagon. He says, [NON-ENGLISH]. Don't be afraid. And they say he should lay down. He laid down, and they shot him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, how I loved my grandfather. He was such a nice man, such a good man. This grandfather of mine would never go-- he-- Thursday before the war and after the war, he made sure that every poor person in our town had what to eat for Shabbat. He would run around and give this one a few zloties and this one a few rubles to make sure that they had food for Shabbat.

speaker: interviewee
text: He brought a-- he found a sefer Torah. He took it to, to Czernowitz and he had it looked over to make sure it's kosher. With a canopy, he made a shul. He was such an nice man. Why did he deserve to die? He just survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, the boys went-- or they sent the-- the Russian police went and they brought his body home. They were crying again. They were burying my grandfather. [SOBBING] My mother and my grandmother and her sisters and brothers are sitting shiva. And we're all crying. We just came out, just a few months. We just survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] A few weeks later, a week later, we hear my father is coming home from the Urals. He's bringing eight Ukrainians with a, a few Russians. And he's coming to get food because there was a famine in the Urals. He's coming to bring food back to the, to the, to the labor camp. We're petrified. We know already what's going on in the, in the woods, that they're all over, the Banderists. We know that his life is in danger, but still you write to him, to the Urals, the letter is not going to get there. The war is going on. It's-- this is '44. This is the end of November, the beginning of December '44. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] And we're mourning my grandfather. December the 30th or the 29th, we figured my father-- a Russian man comes with packages. And he says that my father is a few miles, a few miles away from town. We should send out a horse and wagon-- this is December the 30th, 1944-- that he, he has a lot of heavy packages and that his boots or his shoes hurt, his foot, something. We should send a horse and wagon. Oh, how excited I am! My father is coming home. But my mother is standing, is crying. She's scared. But I, I, I am just excited. I hear my father is coming home. The horse and wagon went out. They never, ever found my father. To this day, we don't know what they did to him, how they killed him. [SOBBING] To this day, we never saw his body. The Ukrainians ran away to join the Banderists. And we're crying again. My mother must have had a nervous breakdown. To this day, when it snows outside, I stand by the window and I watch. And I'm waiting for my father to come home. I'm still waiting. I'm 62 years old. And somehow, I'm still standing like that little child who's waiting. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] God, how I love my father. My mother, my little sister, Sima, we're sitting all together, and we're crying. My mother didn't stop crying for weeks and months. My grandmother used to watch her. She was afraid she should kill herself. And this what we endured after surviving Hitler. We had to lose my father and my grandfather.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was such a nice man. What did he do any harm to anyone? What, do you ask? [SOBBING] You can't understand this now. How do you understand that as a 12-year-old child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I'm already 10 and a half, almost 11 years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: 11-year-old child. The war didn't-- the war didn't end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, how grown up I was. Now I knew I had to start to help my mother. I had to start watching out for my sister, even though my grandmother and my aunts took care of her. But I became my mother's helper. She had that little store. I used to watch they shouldn't steal from her. They shouldn't rob her. Because the Ukrainian women, the peasants, would come. They would come like a bunch, and they would try to, you know-- I became, in 19-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this is-- we're, we're now in the beginning of '45. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In May, the 9th, 1945, the war was over. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Polish citizens were allowed to go back to Poland. This was the pact that Stalin made with Roosevelt and Churchill, that the Polish citizens would be able to go back to Poland. Even those from Siberia, they had to leave, all of them. Also, I forgot to tell you, my Aunt Chana went to Siberia. Her husband was in Siberia. She went to meet her husband, [NON-ENGLISH], in Siberia. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] We took [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] our possessions. We got on cattle trains. And we went to a town with the name of Bytom to Poland. We went with Polish people. While we were on the Ukrainian side, on the Russian side of the border, they were our friends. Once we crossed the Russian border and we came on Polish territory, they started to show their colors, too. We came to Bytom. In Bytom, they had a Jewish agency already.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was Germany, Bytom?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was-- Bytom was Germany, but it was part of Poland, [NON-ENGLISH]. It was Silesia.

speaker: interviewer
text: Silesia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Silesia. And they had already some-- from the Jewish agency, there were people. And, and they, they-- the Red Cross was there. They provided some food for us. And they said that we should start looking for homes. We could-- this was just a receiving place. It was-- it must have been-- or a synagogue or a Jewish theater. It was a very big place. And there were other families, too, from all over our region. People were allowed to leave Russia and to come to Poland. And it seems that Bytom became like a receiving place, a focal point. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] In Bytom, we found out that there is such a thing as an American zone, an English zone, a French zone. My grandmother had two sisters in America. My grandfather had two sisters in America. And my grandmother wrote a letter to one of her sisters. And they got an answer. And they knew that my grandmother was alive. Well, you can imagine how the sisters felt here in the United States. And we said we have to try to get to the American zone. Because in Poland, they had [NON-ENGLISH]. You know, wherever we went, they were killing us.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Kielce, they came in the middle of the night. People that survived-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] people that survived Auschwitz and other places were killed. Can you imagine? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] You survive extermination camps, and at night they came in, and they killed-- they killed a man in Katowitz. And in Bytom, they killed a, a Jewish watchman.

speaker: interviewee
text: They're killing us all over. Maybe we'll come to the United States. Maybe we'll get to, to Germany, to the American zone. Maybe we'll be able to make some kind of a life for ourselves. Because here, we see wherever we go, they're killing us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the grown-ups started to buy papers to go to Germany. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think my uncle went to, to, to Cracow. And they said they were going to buy from the Red Cross. They bought some papers. We got on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In Bytom, we got on the train, and we're going, a big group of people. My aunts and uncles left, like, a week before us. And they, they, they-- and my Uncle Schloime returned already to tell us that he went to save the borders. And he came to get us. And we're, we're, we're on a train. And nobody's bothering us, and we feel that the papers must be good because nobody is bothering us. We come to Czechoslovakia, to Moravska Ostrava. They stop us, and they tell us the papers are no good. We should go back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was a big group of people. We're not the only ones. My Uncle [NON-ENGLISH] came back already from the Siberia with my Aunt Chana. They're on the transport with us together. He pulls a string. He stops the train. We get out of the train. And we tell the Czechoslovakian police, you can kill us right here. We're not moving. We're not going back to the Polish border, across the Polish border, because the [NON-ENGLISH] are waiting there. And they used to rob the Jewish people. Because when the Czechs used to push them back, they used to rob them. And they're going to kill us because they were killing people, too. Anyway, the Red Cross intervenes. They took us into a very big place. Again, we're surrounded, but this time by Czech police. Again, we're, like, in a ghetto, but we're in a house. We're on house arrest. And the Red Cross brings us food. My Uncle Schloime somehow ran away from the transport. He went to-- and he runs back to-- my uncle was already in Linz in Austria in a DP camp. He runs back, and he tells my Uncle Nisel what happened. And Nisel comes back with money because Schloime didn't have that much money with him, tried to buy us out from the Czech police. He comes, and he makes all the arrangements. My sister, Simala, well, she gets the chicken pox or she gets the measles. I don't know which. She's sick. My mother can't travel. Child has temperature, and she had a rash all over her body. And she can't-- my mother's crying again. Again, there is a catastrophe. A woman, [NON-ENGLISH]-- her maiden name was Dodek. Her brother was killed the first one, when we came out from the grotto. [NON-ENGLISH] Dodek was killed. She died. She was under transport with us together. She got a heart attack from all this. When they stopped us, she got a heart attack, and she died in Moravska Ostrava. And her children are crying. They're orphans because the father was killed by the Germans. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My Uncle Nisel comes in through a window. The police let him come in. And he says we have to go, he says. So Yetta, my grandmother, and I and Nisel leave Moravska Ostrava. There were some boys and girls that-- Czech boys, Jewish, from the Briha, I think, or they-- anyway, they came. They bought us the tickets because we didn't speak Czechoslovakian. We spoke Ukrainian, but it's a little bit close to Czechoslovakian. We got on the train, and we come to Prague. In Prague, they had the funkaserne. They had barracks. And there they had a Jewish agency and the Briha. And they had some boys and girls that came from Israel to help. The [NON-ENGLISH] they called them at that time, the survivors of the Holocaust. And we're waiting. At night, we go to-- I remember this. I remember what happened. My grandmother saw I was a very cute little girl. It's not nice to brag, but I was. And she had money. And she took the money, and she padded me with all the money. And she was hoping that they would not search me. And there was-- I had a doll. And she gave me a doll. I had a doll or something and also with money in it. We come to the border. They're just [NON-ENGLISH] police. Search everybody, people that came from Russia, the Polish citizens, you know, that were allowed to leave, people from the concentration camps, everybody. Whatever anyone had, a little ring, a watch, a coat, anything, they take everything away. But we're used to this already.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was the Czech police?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes. This is the end of 1945. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They, they undressed the grown-ups. And me, they let through. In fact, he smiled at me, the policeman. And I knew how to speak Ukrainian, so I said something to him cute. I don't remember what. Maybe I smiled at him. And he let me through. So they did not take away anything from us. We come across the border. And we're walking at night. We have to cross the Czech border to go into Germany. Czech police sees us, and they turn around their heads. They make believe they don't see us because they were bought off from the Briha, from the Jewish agency-- bought them off. And we come across, and we see trucks with young boys and girls. And they take us in the trucks. Before they take us in the trucks, they put, they spray, like, white powder on us. They gave us, like, a disinfection because they were afraid people shouldn't bring TB or something. And they take us to Munich. In Munich, we embark. And they tell us there is such a thing as DP camps. We knew already that they had this place. And my grandmother says-- Displaced Persons Camps. My grandmother says that she has a sister that left a, a week or two before, that my uncle had told her she's in Fohrenwald. And we go to Fohrenwald. My sister and my mother are still in Czechoslovakia. We come to Fohrenwald, and my grandmother's sister, [NON-ENGLISH] is there with her family. We come in. We see it's one big room. And about three, four families are living in the room with-- divided, like material or rags were divided, like sheets divided the beds. We come in, and they tell us that here they have a school, and they, and they have organizations, and they have, they give-- there's a big kitchen. You have to stay in line. You get food.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the morning-- I get up very early in the morning, and I look for school. That was my first thing. I want an education. I come to school. I see there are other girls there, meydl, little girls. I said, meydl-- in Jewish I speak to them. What are you doing here? They said, we have a school. I says, where do you live? They asked me, do you have father? I said, no, my father was killed. And where is your mother? I said, my mother is in Czechoslovakia. She was detained. We were separated. She says, listen, we have a home. Come with us. From school, I go to the home. Come in, there is a home, a kinderheim, children's home. Come into the heim, to the home, two ladies are standing there. One was [NON-ENGLISH] and one was [NON-ENGLISH]. And they were accustomed to this because children used to bring other children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We children used to find other children. Isn't that something?

speaker: interviewee
text: She says to me, shalom, come in. We have room for you. We have a bed for you. Don't be afraid. There are other children like you. That's when I really started to live like a child. They had bathtubs there. The American women from the joint distribution or from UNRRA used to bring food. They took care of us. I started to flourish. Here I learned how to speak-- they used to speak to us in Hebrew. They were training us to go to Israel. And in school, I was good. Six months later, my mother came legally. The Red Cross allowed them to come. And my mother came to Fohrenwald, to the DP camp. The camp was overcrowded. And my sister looked terrible. I came to the home, and I begged them they should take my sister in. And they did. And there was no room for her, so there was a crib. I put a crib next to my little cot. And I put my sister, Sima, into the crib. But she used to eat well. And I'll show you later pictures how we sit and we eat. We had food. I was looking for nourishment for my sister. Because in the DP camp, you know, the, the food that everyone got was not as good as the food that the children got. Because the children, we were looked after like-- we were like their diamonds that they found. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Anyway, the orphanage is going on. We're already in 1946, the end of '46. The beginning of '47, the orphanage, the children have to register-- or to go to Israel or to go to America. They have a choice. They're not forced to go to Israel. They are given a choice. Because some of them remember that their parents had relatives in America. So they are given a choice. I decide I'm going to Israel. I joined the Betar. My father belonged to the Betar. I'm going to Israel. And my mother also thought perhaps she'll go to a kibbutz. She's a widow with two small children. And she--

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue in a second. OK.
